text: I volunteer to chair committees or working groups without hesitation.
I prefer to serve on committees rather than chair them.
I am the first to speak up when a group needs someone to take ownership of a problem.
I look for ways to distribute ownership of problems across the group.
I notice that I instinctively assign myself the most critical tasks.
I notice that I instinctively ask others which tasks they feel best suited for.
I often reshape a project's scope based on my own assessment of what matters.
I let the group collectively decide when a project's scope should change.
I feel most effective when I can direct the allocation of responsibilities.
I feel most effective when responsibilities are negotiated openly among team members.
I am quick to declare a path forward when the group is circling.
I trust that the group will eventually resolve its own circular discussions.
I enjoy the pressure that comes with being the person in charge.
I enjoy the freedom that comes with not being the person in charge.
When a conversation stalls, I push it forward with a concrete proposal.
When a conversation stalls, I invite quieter voices to share before proposing anything.
I feel that my role in groups is to cut through noise and create clarity.
I feel that my role in groups is to ensure everyone feels included in the process.
I am the one who typically opens a meeting by framing the key question.
I am the one who typically helps the group settle on the key question together.
I would rather make a controversial call than let the team drift without direction.
I would rather let the team take extra time than force a call that divides people.
I feel that establishing hierarchy early prevents confusion later.
I feel that establishing equal footing early strengthens collaboration later.
I naturally gravitate toward being the person who introduces people and assigns them roles.
I naturally gravitate toward connecting people and letting them find their own roles.
I am comfortable interrupting a group discussion to redirect its focus.
I let group discussions find their own focus, even if they meander for a while.
I find it hard to step aside when I believe I am the most capable person to lead.
I step aside willingly to give others opportunities to lead, even if I feel capable.
I feel a surge of energy when I declare a new direction for a team.
I feel a surge of energy when a team collectively discovers its new direction.
I proactively recruit people to my initiatives.
I wait to be invited into other people's initiatives.
I prefer to be the one who decides when a meeting ends.
I prefer that the group collectively decides when a meeting has run its course.
I am the person who usually says, 'Here is what we are going to do.'
I am the person who usually says, 'What does everyone think we should do?'
I find it rewarding to have the final word in a discussion.
I find it rewarding when a discussion closes with genuine shared understanding.
I readily step in to mediate disputes by imposing a resolution.
I help mediate disputes by drawing out each side's concerns without imposing a resolution.
I consider it my strength that I can make hard calls quickly.
I consider it my strength that I can hold space for difficult conversations.
I am drawn to founding organizations rather than joining existing ones.
I am drawn to strengthening organizations that already exist.
I tend to unilaterally escalate issues when I think a group is underreacting.
I tend to check with the group before escalating any issue.
I naturally assume the authority to speak on behalf of a group.
I ask the group's permission before speaking on its behalf.
I set milestones for the team and hold everyone to them.
I help the team set milestones together and support everyone in reaching them.
I believe that someone who takes command earns the right to shape the outcome.
I believe that outcomes belong to the whole group, regardless of who takes command.
I am typically the one who breaks a tie in a group vote.
I am typically the one who helps the group keep deliberating until a tie resolves itself.
I feel most capable when I have unilateral authority over a domain.
I feel most capable when authority is shared across team members in a domain.
I am inclined to announce decisions first and explain my reasoning second.
I am inclined to share my reasoning first and let the group reach the decision.
I find myself naturally framing situations as problems for me to solve.
I find myself naturally framing situations as challenges for us to solve together.
I enjoy negotiating from a position of authority.
I enjoy negotiating as an equal partner.
I tend to set the criteria by which the group evaluates options.
I tend to let the group define its own evaluation criteria.
I am the kind of person who writes the first draft and circulates it for comment.
I am the kind of person who waits for a first draft and then helps improve it.
I feel a need to put my stamp on everything I am involved in.
I feel comfortable contributing to something that bears the group's stamp, not mine.
I quickly identify who should do what and communicate it directly.
I ask people what they would like to take on rather than assigning tasks.
I usually frame debates by stating my position clearly at the start.
I usually enter debates by asking questions rather than stating positions.
I instinctively protect my decision-making authority from interference.
I instinctively invite others into my decision-making process.
I am the one who cuts off brainstorming to move the group into execution.
I let brainstorming run its natural course before anyone pushes toward execution.
I like to personally approve important outputs before they go out.
I trust the group's collective review process without needing personal approval authority.
I see my presence in a group as primarily about providing direction.
I see my presence in a group as primarily about providing support.
I feel compelled to step in and correct others when they are off track.
I gently offer perspective but leave it to others to decide if they are off track.
I am energized by pitching my vision to stakeholders.
I am energized by helping a group articulate its shared vision to stakeholders.
I prefer to control the narrative around a project's progress.
I prefer that the narrative around a project's progress reflects everyone's perspective.
I am the one who decides when to pivot and when to stay the course.
I believe pivot-or-persist decisions should be made by the group together.
I feel at home being the primary representative of a group to the outside world.
I feel at home rotating representation duties among group members.
When entering a new group, I quickly establish my role as a leader.
When entering a new group, I observe how things work before settling into a role.
I take pride in having built teams from scratch.
I take pride in helping existing teams become more effective.
I would rather risk overstepping than under-contributing in a crisis.
I would rather check in with others than risk overstepping in a crisis.
I am comfortable telling someone their approach needs to change.
I prefer to ask questions that help someone see for themselves whether their approach is working.
I frequently take on the role of gatekeeper for quality in group work.
I prefer that quality standards be maintained through peer review rather than a single gatekeeper.
I feel that my decisiveness prevents wasted effort.
I feel that inclusive deliberation prevents misguided effort.
I have a tendency to take over tasks that others are doing too slowly.
I have a tendency to offer help rather than take over when someone is struggling.
I feel most like myself when I am running the show.
I feel most like myself when I am part of an ensemble.
I would rather give a keynote than participate in a panel discussion.
I would rather participate in a panel discussion than give a keynote alone.
I notice that people look to me to set the standard of effort in a group.
I notice that I calibrate my effort to match the group's established rhythm.
I am comfortable unilaterally canceling plans that I think are not working.
I bring concerns about a plan to the group rather than canceling it myself.
I am usually the first to stand up and take charge in a new situation.
I am usually the first to check in with the group before anyone takes charge.
I feel that once I commit to a direction, others should follow.
I feel that committing to a direction should be a collective act.
I tend to hold the pen on important documents and shape the final version.
I tend to let multiple people contribute to important documents without one person holding the pen.
I enjoy being the person who approves or vetoes ideas.
I enjoy being part of a group that evaluates ideas together without veto power.
I instinctively create a chain of command when I work with others.
I instinctively resist hierarchy and prefer flat collaboration.
I prefer to be the one who communicates bad news to the team.
I prefer that bad news be communicated by whoever is closest to the situation, not a single leader.
I see group work as an opportunity to shape people's priorities.
I see group work as an opportunity to learn from other people's priorities.
I feel uncomfortable when someone else is making decisions that I believe should be mine.
I feel comfortable deferring decisions to others, even in areas I know well.
I often end up being the one who holds others accountable for their commitments.
I believe the group should hold each other accountable rather than one person doing it.
I am comfortable issuing ultimatums when I think they are needed.
I avoid ultimatums and prefer to keep negotiating until everyone is on board.
I would rather lead a risky venture than follow in a safe one.
I would rather participate in a safe venture than lead a risky one alone.
I naturally claim the most visible and consequential role in any effort.
I naturally gravitate toward the role where I can best support others' efforts.
I feel that ambiguity is a signal for me to step in and create order.
I feel that ambiguity is an invitation for the group to explore possibilities together.
I prefer to set the ground rules before any group activity begins.
I prefer that ground rules emerge from group discussion before activity begins.
I am the person who usually decides how resources get distributed in a group project.
I prefer that the group decides collectively how resources get distributed.
I tend to be the voice that names the elephant in the room.
I tend to wait and see if the group will name the elephant in the room on its own.
I get restless in meetings where no one is driving toward a conclusion.
I am patient in meetings that take longer than expected, trusting the process.
I find it natural to give performance feedback without being asked.
I offer feedback only when asked or when the group has a feedback culture in place.
I am wired to fix what is broken rather than wait for someone else to notice.
I am wired to flag what seems broken and let the group decide how to address it.
I consider it a compliment when people describe me as commanding.
I consider it a compliment when people describe me as easygoing.
I naturally position myself at the center of decision-making networks.
I naturally position myself as a connector at the periphery, linking others together.
I am the person who reframes a problem so the team can act on it.
I am the person who asks the team how they would like to frame a problem.
I feel that good leadership means making the call, not facilitating the call.
I feel that good leadership means creating conditions for the group to make the call.
I am comfortable vetoing a group decision when I believe it is wrong.
I am uncomfortable using veto power, even when I think I see a better path.
I prefer to be the single point of contact for important communications.
I prefer that important communications come from the group rather than a single person.
I feel that my presence in a group naturally organizes the group around my priorities.
I feel that my presence in a group helps others articulate their own priorities.
I actively lobby for my perspective before a group makes a decision.
I share my perspective and then genuinely let the group weigh all viewpoints equally.
I am the type who creates the agenda, not the type who follows one.
I am the type who contributes thoughtfully to an agenda someone else has set.
I tend to scope out my territory in any collaborative effort.
I tend to blur territorial lines in favor of fluid collaboration.
I am drawn to being the person who makes the toughest calls.
I am drawn to being the person who helps the group make tough calls collectively.
I feel that my strength lies in driving momentum.
I feel that my strength lies in sustaining group cohesion.
I commonly take the initiative to close out projects and declare them complete.
I prefer the group to decide together when a project is truly complete.
I feel responsible for ensuring that meetings have concrete outcomes.
I feel that meetings can be valuable even without concrete outcomes, as long as the group connected.
I like to be the one who determines the scope of what the team works on.
I like when the scope of work is something the whole team defines together.
I find that I end up directing the work even when it is not my formal role.
I find that I end up supporting the work quietly even when I could direct it.
I am quick to assert my expertise when a topic I know well comes up.
I offer my expertise gently and only when it seems helpful, not as an assertion of authority.
I prefer to set the timeline for a group effort rather than negotiate it.
I prefer that timelines be negotiated among everyone involved.
I often rewrite others' contributions to match my vision.
I honor others' contributions and integrate them with minimal changes.
I feel energized when I single-handedly turn a situation around.
I feel energized when the group collectively turns a situation around.
I am comfortable pulling rank to resolve a disagreement.
I prefer to resolve disagreements through dialogue rather than any form of rank.
I consider it part of my job to mentor others by directing their growth.
I consider it part of my job to support others by walking alongside their growth.
I am the type who builds the team around my vision rather than the other way around.
I am the type who shapes my contribution around the team's existing vision.
I enjoy the sense of being indispensable to a project's direction.
I enjoy knowing that a project can succeed regardless of whether I am personally driving it.
I make a point of having the last say before a group commits to a course of action.
I am content letting the group commit without needing to add my final word.
I naturally consolidate decision-making power as projects progress.
I naturally distribute decision-making power more broadly as projects progress.
I feel most useful when I am calling the shots under pressure.
I feel most useful when I am steadying the group under pressure.
I am comfortable making commitments on behalf of my team without consulting them first.
I always consult with my team before making commitments that involve them.
I am the person who pushes for a vote when discussion drags on.
I am the person who advocates for more discussion when others want to rush a vote.
I feel that leading well means deciding on behalf of those who cannot yet see the full picture.
I feel that leading well means ensuring everyone has access to the full picture.
I frequently reorganize a group's workflow to align with my approach.
I adapt my approach to align with the group's existing workflow.
I tend to define what done looks like before anyone else weighs in.
I ask the group to define what done looks like before we begin.
I like to personally onboard new members and set their expectations.
I like when the group collectively onboards new members.
I am recognized for my ability to rally people behind a single vision.
I am recognized for my ability to weave diverse viewpoints into a cohesive group effort.
I prefer to own the relationship with external partners on behalf of the team.
I prefer that the team shares relationships with external partners.
I am drawn to moments where one person's decision changes everything.
I am drawn to moments where a group's collective effort changes everything.
I find it easy to overrule suggestions that do not align with my direction.
I find it difficult to overrule suggestions, even when they do not align with my own thinking.
I quickly claim the most strategic position in any group effort.
I happily take whatever position best supports the group's needs.
I often speak with an air of finality that signals the discussion is over.
I speak in a way that signals the discussion remains open for others to shape.
I typically introduce structure to group work before anyone else does.
I typically let someone else introduce structure and then adapt to it.
I want to be the one who launches new ventures within my team.
I want to support whoever launches new ventures within my team.
I find it satisfying to have direct reports and be responsible for their output.
I find it satisfying to work as part of a peer network without reporting lines.
I am the person who sets the agenda for one-on-one conversations.
I let one-on-one conversations flow according to both people's interests.
I enjoy the challenge of getting skeptics to follow my lead.
I enjoy the challenge of finding common ground with skeptics through mutual respect.
I am the one who defines success criteria for the group.
I am the one who asks the group to co-create success criteria.
I feel confident imposing boundaries when collaboration gets off track.
I raise concerns when collaboration gets off track but leave it to the group to set boundaries.
I prefer to personally handle the most high-stakes elements of any project.
I prefer that high-stakes elements be handled by whoever the group deems best suited.
I am energized by standing at the front of the room and guiding the discussion.
I am energized by being in the circle and contributing as one voice among many.
I consider my ability to command attention an important professional asset.
I consider my ability to amplify others' voices an important professional asset.
I tend to make executive decisions about scope changes mid-project.
I tend to consult the team before making any scope changes mid-project.
I feel that groups need a strong anchor, and I provide that.
I feel that groups anchor themselves when every member has genuine influence.
I am usually the one who interviews and selects new team members.
I prefer that new team members be selected by the whole team together.
I tend to present a completed analysis rather than share my work in progress for input.
I tend to share my work in progress early so others can shape it with me.
I feel that taking initiative without waiting for permission is a sign of strength.
I feel that seeking input before acting is a sign of strength.
I quickly establish the pecking order in any new group I join.
I resist pecking orders and advocate for egalitarian group dynamics.
I would rather be the one steering than the one rowing.
I am happy to row in sync with everyone else rather than steer alone.
I enjoy naming the problem and telling the group how we will solve it.
I enjoy helping the group name the problem together and co-create a solution.
I am inclined to enforce standards even when the group has not explicitly agreed to them.
I enforce only standards the group has explicitly agreed to.
I enjoy shaping the culture of any team I join.
I enjoy adapting to and reinforcing the culture that already exists on a team.
I feel most in my element when I am the person who makes the go or no-go call.
I feel most in my element when the go or no-go call is a genuine group decision.
I instinctively place myself in the center of the information flow.
I instinctively work to make sure information flows freely to everyone, not through me.
I enjoy being the gatekeeper who controls access to key decisions.
I work to ensure that access to key decisions is open to everyone involved.
I am comfortable making big bets on behalf of a group.
I prefer that big bets be discussed thoroughly with the group before anyone commits.
I often proactively define the criteria for who gets to participate in key discussions.
I believe key discussions should be open to anyone who wants to participate.
I see my job as pointing the group in the right direction and clearing the path.
I see my job as walking the path alongside the group, wherever it leads.
I feel uncomfortable when someone challenges my authority in front of the group.
I welcome challenges to my suggestions because they strengthen the group's thinking.
I tend to be the one who says the meeting is over and assigns next steps.
I tend to let the group decide when the meeting wraps and what the next steps are.
I am drawn to the captain's chair, not the crew's quarters.
I am drawn to a close-knit crew that steers the ship together.
I feel that speed of execution justifies centralized decision-making.
I feel that buy-in justifies taking extra time for collective decision-making.
I commonly find myself the one editing and finalizing group deliverables.
I prefer that group deliverables be finalized by whoever the group entrusts with that task.
I feel a strong pull to shape the agenda of any group I am part of.
I feel a strong pull to ensure the agenda reflects everyone's interests, not just mine.
I rarely second-guess a directive I have given, even under pressure.
I am open to revisiting any decision if the group raises new concerns.
I am the one who usually initiates the post-mortem and names what went wrong.
I prefer that post-mortems be a shared reflection rather than one person's assessment.
I feel that my willingness to take command in uncertain moments is what makes me effective.
I feel that my willingness to let go of control in uncertain moments is what makes the group effective.
